text.title: Model Registry, Versioning, and Checkpoint Lifecycle
text.summary: Fine-tuned model ID registry management, checkpoint expiry policy, cost tracking, and operational patterns for long-term production maintenance of fine-tuned models.

rule.statement: Store fine-tuned model IDs in a config file or database with metadata: base_model, dataset_version, n_epochs, training_date, test_score.
rule.rationale: OpenAI model IDs are opaque strings (ft:gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18:org::abc123) that are impossible to manage at scale without a registry. The org slug in the ID becomes invalid if the organization changes.

rule.statement: Monitor fine-tuned model checkpoints for the 90-day inactivity expiry — OpenAI expires checkpoints after 90 days without API calls; retrain before expiry for production models.
rule.rationale: Expired checkpoints cannot be recovered; production systems must track checkpoint health and proactively retrain or make periodic inference calls to prevent expiry.

rule.statement: Estimate training token cost before upload with tiktoken: total_tokens * n_epochs * cost_per_1M.
rule.rationale: A large dataset at 3 epochs with gpt-4o pricing ($25/1M) can cost hundreds of dollars; agents must surface this estimate to humans before submitting.

example.note: Always show cost estimate to a human before auto-submitting a job.

rule.statement: Always create a separate validation file (10% of training data) and pass validation_file to the job — OpenAI reports validation loss in job events.
rule.rationale: Without a validation file, there is no way to detect overfitting during training; OpenAI reports validation loss only when a validation file is provided.
